summary:
## How the game went

Out of the Queen's Gambit Declined opening, the first nine moves were theory — but then Miromisan (1312) went on an adventure with **10...Na6**, a risky knight sortie to the edge of the board that immediately cost Black ~15% winning chances. You punished it instantly and accurately: **11.Bxa6** snapped off the offside knight, vaulting your winning probability to 70%. The midgame saw you consolidate confidently with **13.Bd2** and the powerful **14.Ne5** (82 seconds well spent), maintaining a dominant structure. By moves 17–18, the game was essentially over: Miromisan's **17...Rab8** and **18...Be4** — both times missing the better defensive try f6 — collapsed Black's position, and resignation followed on move 20.

---

## Strengths

**Pouncing on the adventurous knight.** When the opponent played the dubious 10...Na6, you didn't hesitate — **11.Bxa6** (7.7s, win% 70.1) was both Best and natural for your level. Taking free material cleanly is not trivial; many players at this rating waffle or misorder captures.

**Central control with Ne5.** After your accuracy dipped at move 12, you righted the ship with **13.Bd2** and **14.Ne5** back-to-back. The Ne5 jump (win% 69.9) re-established piece activity and showed good positional instinct — even after spending 82 seconds on it.

**Clean finish.** Once Miromisan misplayed the endgame with 17...Rab8 and 18...Be4, your win% shot from ~45% to ~89% in two moves. You converted without letting the opponent back in — a 20-move win by resignation with 93.3% accuracy is a disciplined performance.

---

## Weaknesses

**12.Be2 — the missed queen activation.** This is the game's clearest missed opportunity. With 75 seconds on the clock you chose 12.Be2, dropping your win% by 16 points (to 55.9%). The engine preferred **Qa4**, with the idea Qa4–Qb3 trading queens while keeping Black's queenside tangled. Only 2% of players at your level find this — it's genuinely hard — but the key takeaway is: after winning material with Bxa6, the question should have been "how do I activate my queen to squeeze the advantage?" rather than a quiet bishop retreat.

**15.Bh5 — a costly bishop lunge.** This was your bigger inaccuracy and the sharpest swing of the game: win% fell from ~70% to 43.5%, erasing most of the advantage you'd built. The correct idea was **Qb3**, immediately pressuring b7 and the queenside pawns (the line runs Qb3 Qxb3 axb3 Ne4 Be1 f6 Nd3 Ng5). Moving the bishop to h5 looks aggressive but lost the thread. Notably, you spent 58.8 seconds here — so the time pressure wasn't the culprit; this was a conceptual miscalculation about where the winning plan lay.

---

## What to train

**1. Converting a material advantage with queen activity.** Both inaccuracies (12.Be2 and 15.Bh5) share a common root: after winning a piece, you fell back to passive/speculative bishop moves rather than activating your queen to put concrete pressure on weaknesses. Practice positions where you're up material and must find the most forcing queen centralisation before the opponent can reorganise.

**2. Recognising the "active retreat."** The Qa4–Qb3 idea (after 12.) and the Qb3 squeeze (after 15.) are both examples of an "active queen post" — moves that look like nothing but immediately fix targets. These moves are found by only 2–5% of players at your level, which explains why they're hard. Drill endgame/middlegame queen-activation exercises to build this pattern recognition.

**3. Punishing rim-knight sorties.** You handled 10...Na6 perfectly this game, but it's worth cementing the general principle: when the opponent places a knight on a6 or h6 in a closed pawn structure, the refutation is typically immediate capture or a pawn advance to trap it, not delay. Your clean 11.Bxa6 at 7.7 seconds shows you already have the instinct — reinforce it so it stays automatic under time pressure in faster games.

summary:
## How the game went

You took Black in a Queen's Pawn Game against whiizzy and navigated the opening with impressive calm — five book moves, then a series of Best choices including the "coolheaded" 7...Be7 that kept tension under control. The midgame turned twice: at move 11 you spent 91 seconds and still played the Miss 11...Bd3 instead of the incisive Ne4, letting a potentially decisive advantage slip. Your opponent meanwhile misfired with 17.Ne1 (missing Ne5) and 23.Qa5 (missing Ncb4), giving the position back to you on a plate. The finish was chaotic — your 25...Qb5 was a Blunder burned on just 5.5 seconds, but whiizzy immediately self-destructed with 26.Nxa7 (win% 0.1%), and you delivered checkmate on move 26.

---

## Strengths

- **Opening solidity.** Five book moves, then 6...e6, 7...Be7, and 9...O-O were all Best — you entered the middlegame on equal or better terms without gifting anything.
- **Calm under pressure.** The "coolheaded" tag on 7...Be7 is well-earned; you didn't panic or overreach early.
- **Positional recapture.** 12...cxd3 (Best) kept the structure sensible when a worse player might have grabbed material recklessly.
- **Accuracy edge.** Your 85.8% accuracy beat whiizzy's 81.5% — you were the more consistent player across 26 moves.

---

## Weaknesses

**1. The 91-second Miss on move 11 (11...Bd3).** This is the most glaring moment. You used nearly a minute and a half and landed on the wrong plan. The engine's recommendation, Ne4, initiates a knight maneuver — Ne4 gxf5 Nxd2 Kxd2 Nf6 — that would have swung the win probability by nearly 50 points. When you spend that much time, you need to find the forcing line, not the quiet-looking piece placement.

**2. Hasty Blunder at move 25 (25...Qb5, 5.5 seconds).** The contrast to move 11 is stark: there you burned time and missed; here you spent almost nothing and blundered. Win% dropped 43 points. The correct recapture bxc6 — leading to Bxd6 Re8 Bb4 — would have consolidated cleanly. In a winning position, slowing down to double-check captures is exactly what separates a clean win from a scare.

**3. Structural hesitation at move 17 (17...g6).** Even spending 55 seconds, you went for g6 instead of the pawn-winning Bxa3 (Qc2 Rc4 bxa3 Rfc8 Kd2 Rxc3). Maia confirms this is hard — only 11% of players at your level find it — but it's worth noting that a direct material-winning idea was available and you chose a passive defensive move instead.

**4. Rook misplacement at move 18 (18...Rc6 vs. Rc4).** An inaccuracy that cost ~11% win probability. Only 3% find Rc4 here, so it's understandable, but the pattern — centralizing a rook actively vs. semi-passively — is a recurring theme in your middlegame.

---

## What to train

**1. Knight outpost tactics (Ne4-type maneuvers).** Move 11 showed you didn't see — or didn't trust — a knight leap into the center. Study positions where a knight on e4 or d5 creates structural damage and forces king movement. The line Ne4 gxf5 Nxd2 Kxd2 is a classic idea: trade a knight for maximum disruption. Doing 20–30 tactical puzzles specifically on knight outposts and sacrifice-into-king-walk patterns will help this click faster under the clock.

**2. Tempo discipline: match your clock to the position's danger level.** You spent 91 seconds on move 11 (low urgency, early middlegame) and 5.5 seconds on move 25 (critical, near-winning endgame). That's inverted. Practice the habit of asking: *"Is this a forcing moment?"* If yes, slow down. If you're in a quiet maneuvering phase, trust your instincts and move sooner. Your overall 21.1s average is healthy — you just need to redistribute it.

**3. Active rook placement in the middlegame.** Both 18...Rc6 (should be Rc4) and the theme in whiizzy's best line Rc4 Kb1 Rfc8 point to the same lesson: your rooks want open files and active squares, not passive defense. Spend time with rook endgame and middlegame studies where the correct idea is always "put the rook where it pressures something," not "keep the rook safe." The Rc4 idea — doubling on the c-file — appeared as the right answer twice in this game.

summary:
## How the game went

The Queen's Gambit Declined: Harrwitz Attack opened with four book moves from both sides, then exploded at move 6. VaRrOa_DeStRuCtOr played 6.cxd5 — a serious blunder that immediately handed you a 62.6% winning position. But you answered with 6...exd5, surrendering nearly all of that gift back; Nxd5 was the correct recapture, and with it Stockfish's line (Nxd5 Bd2 Nxd4 Qc1 Qe7 e3 Nxc3 Bxc3) would have left you winning comfortably at 83%. You then navigated the middlegame cleanly — O-O, Bd6, bxc6, and f6 were all best moves — until move 25, when your opponent handed you a second gift: 25.a4 collapsed white's winning chances to just 28.5%, giving you a 71.5% position again. Critically, moves 35 and 38 were the real killers: Rhg4 and Raf4+ (both blunders) let two nearly won positions slip away, and after 41...Kg5 the position was essentially beyond saving at 7.3%, prompting the resignation on move 42.

---

## Strengths

**Accurate middlegame technique.** Your play from moves 8 through roughly 30 was genuinely good — 16 best moves and 5 excellent moves in the game record reflects a player who found the right ideas most of the time. Bd6 (move 11, tagged *coolheaded*) and f6 (move 14, spent 51 seconds) show you were thinking concretely rather than reacting, and both are praised by Stockfish.

**Equal accuracy under pressure.** Finishing at 86.6% — identical to your higher-rated opponent — means you weren't outplayed conceptually. VaRrOa_DeStRuCtOr made just as many errors; you simply weren't able to punish them.

**Solid opening preparation.** Four book moves and then a sequence that reached a perfectly playable position shows familiarity with the QGD structure.

---

## Weaknesses

**You returned two large gifts.** The defining pattern of this game is receiving a winning advantage and not converting it — twice. After 6.cxd5 your position was 62.6% winning; 6...exd5 dropped you to 46%. After 25.a4 you were back to 71.5% winning; a cascade of rook blunders on moves 35 and 38 threw it all away. The move that hurts most is 35...Rhg4 — Stockfish notes that 69% of players at your level find Rxa4 here, so this was a knowable move, not an inhuman calculation. With Rxa4, the line Rxa4 Qc2+ Rae4 Rb3 f5 gives you 86.8% winning chances; you got 24.7% instead. A 62-point swing on a move most peers find.

**Rook coordination in the endgame.** Move 38...Raf4+ instead of Rxa6 is the same pattern: your rooks were active but aimed at the wrong targets. Rxa6 followed by Rf4+ Ke3 Rf8 keeps material and keeps winning chances at 86.4%; Raf4+ dropped you to 31.4%. Both 35 and 38 suggest a habit of going for checks and activity over material when the position actually called for consolidating your advantage.

**31...Qe1+ (the Miss).** In an already difficult position you played Qe1+, missing Qh2+ Kf1 Rhe4 Rh3 Rf4+ Rf3, which would have held the position at a much more manageable losing probability. The miss cost roughly 43 percentage points of winning chances. When your position is already under pressure, queen activity needs to be genuinely dangerous — a check that leads nowhere is a tempo handed to your opponent.

---

## What to train

**1. Recapture direction in the Queen's Gambit (Nxd5 vs exd5).** The 6...exd5 blunder is a classic QGD structural decision. Recapturing with the knight keeps a piece on d5, controls the center dynamically, and avoids giving white a free initiative; recapturing with the pawn cedes central tension cheaply. Spend time with QGD positions specifically on this decision — not just memorizing the answer but understanding *why* Nxd5 is dominant here (the knight becomes a strong outpost that white must spend time dislodging).

**2. Converting winning rook endgames — prioritize material over activity.** Moves 35 and 38 both show the same error: choosing an "active" rook move (checks, pin attempts) when the position called for capturing a loose pawn and simplifying toward a won endgame. Drill rook endgames where you are up material or have a passed pawn — the habit of asking "can I just take something?" before looking for combinations will catch both of these cases.

**3. Calculation at critical junctures — slow down on rook moves.** You spent 44 seconds on 35...Rhg4 (still wrong) but only 9.8 seconds on 38...Raf4+ (also wrong). Rook moves are the hardest piece to visualize because they cover entire files and ranks; hasty rook decisions are where games at this level are most often lost. When you have two rooks active and a winning position, force yourself to compare at least two candidate moves before committing — specifically "take the material" vs "create threats."

summary:
## How the Game Went

You opened with the Queen's Gambit and ran into the McDonnell Defense — and almost gave away the early advantage yourself: **4.d5** was a blunder that cost you 12.2% win probability, letting the opponent equalize or better. The critical turning point came at move 7: matbcp replied with **7...Bb4**, missing the correct **Nxd5** and instantly swinging the position 32% in your favor. You punished it immediately and accurately — **8.Qa4+**, **9.Bxc6+**, **10.Qxb4** in rapid succession — winning the bishop pair and material while the opponent's pieces scrambled. By move 15 the advantage was substantial, and after matbcp compounded the damage with **15...O-O** (missing the defensive **Bb5**), the game was essentially over structurally. You converted a 92%+ winning position through the endgame, and the opponent resigned on move 61.

## Strengths

You spotted the tactical sequence starting with **8.Qa4+** instantly and played it with confidence — all three moves (Qa4+, Bxc6+, Qxb4) rated Great or Best with sub-2-second decision times. That's clean, decisive tactical vision. Your overall move quality was excellent: 19 Excellent, 17 Best, 14 Good out of 61 moves, and you closed out a winning endgame against a higher-rated opponent without letting them back in. The 94.2% accuracy on 61 moves in a rapid game is genuinely strong.

## Weaknesses

**4.d5** stands out as the clearest concrete error — played in only 6.4 seconds in the opening, where precision matters most. The engine recommends **Nf3**, developing naturally and keeping tension; pushing d5 prematurely conceded positional ground you had to claw back. **15.Re1** was the second meaningful miss: the stronger **Qxa6** (the line: Qxa6 cxd5 Qa3 Qb4 Qxb4 Rxb4) trades queens and simplifies into a clearly winning endgame — but admittedly this is a move only 0% of players at your level find, so it's a ceiling, not a scandal. More concerning is **43.Rg6**, played in just 1.6 seconds with the game already won (92.5%): **f3** was correct. Rushing in a winning position is a recurring risk — it's where unnecessary complications creep in.

## What to Train

1. **Opening move-order discipline (d-pawn pushes).** 4.d5 is the kind of move that feels active but releases central tension prematurely. In QGA structures, spend a little more time before committing pawns — the extra 10 seconds on move 4 would have been well spent. Study when d4-d5 is a threat vs. a concession.

2. **Tactical alertness after you're already winning.** Move 43 (Rg6, 1.6s) is the clearest symptom: when you have a 92% position, the danger is complacency, not complexity. Practice finishing won endgames — specifically rook+knight vs. rook endgames — to build the habit of calculating even "easy" moves.

3. **Queen activation before rook moves.** At move 15, Re1 is a natural-looking developing move, but Qxa6 was the engine's decisive improvement — the queen belonged in the thick of it, not the rook. In your middlegame training, ask yourself *"can my queen do something concrete right now?"* before reflexively activating a rook.

summary:
## How the Game Went

You opened with the Caro-Kann as Black against GoldenNinjaWarrior (1493), navigating the early moves largely on book knowledge — 8 book moves in total. On move 9 you played **9...Bc5**, an inaccuracy that let a small edge slip; the engine preferred **9...Ne7**, a tricky re-routing only 5% of players at your rating find. You recovered immediately: **10...Ne7** — played after a thoughtful 32 seconds — was judged Best, stabilising the position and keeping the balance near even. The real turning point came on move 12 when your opponent panicked with **12.b4**, a blunder (win% collapsed from ~41% to 25%) — instead of the solid **12.O-O**, they lunged at your bishop and handed you a free pawn. You punished it instantly: **12...Bxb4** in under a second, a natural and powerful reply. From there you kept converting: **16...Rc8**, an above-your-level rook activation that only 7% at your rating find, tightened the vice, and by move 20 **20...Nxe7** kept the pressure mounting until your opponent resigned on move 23.

---

## Strengths

**Accuracy under pressure.** A 94% accuracy rate against a higher-rated opponent in a sharp position is genuinely impressive — you outplayed them by nearly 9 percentage points.

**Instant exploitation.** You captured on b4 in 0.8 seconds, showing you'd already calculated the consequences of their pawn thrust. No hesitation when the gift arrived.

**Rook activation.** **16...Rc8** is the kind of prophylactic, pressure-building rook move that stronger players play almost automatically but eludes most 1300–1400 players. Finding it here, even after 82 seconds of thought, marks real positional instinct.

**Recovery after the inaccuracy.** After 9...Bc5, you didn't panic or drift — 10...Ne7 was the exact repair, and you found it on the very next move.

---

## Weaknesses

**9...Bc5 — one tempo off the ideal square.** The engine wanted **9...Ne7**, heading toward **Ne7–g6** with tempo and flexibility (the line shows ...Nbc6, ...Ng6 coordinating naturally). Your bishop on c5 isn't badly placed, but it gave White an extra option to target it, and the win probability dipped 7.8 percentage points. That said, this is a genuinely hard move to find — only 5% of your peers choose it — so the criticism is mild. The bigger lesson is pattern recognition: in the Caro-Kann, the knight re-route via e7 appears in many lines and is worth memorising.

**No other significant errors.** Two inaccuracies, zero blunders, zero misses. The game was controlled from the moment the opponent overextended. There's no catastrophic hole to fix here — the concern is raising the ceiling, not patching the floor.

---

## What to Train

**1. The Ne7–Ng6 knight manoeuvre in the Caro-Kann.**
Move 9 showed you defaulted to bishop development when the knight re-route was stronger. This is one of the signature ideas in the Classical and Advance Caro-Kann — spend 20–30 minutes replaying master games where Black plays ...Ne7–g6 and notice how it coordinates with the bishop pair. Once it's a pattern you *recognise*, you won't spend time on Bc5 when Ne7 is available.

**2. Rook-to-open-file timing.**
16...Rc8 was above your level — but it took 82 seconds to find. The underlying idea (activate the rook to a half-open or contested file before converting a material advantage) is something you *can* do, as this game proves. Practice endgame and middlegame positions specifically asking: "Which rook, which file, right now?" until the decision feels reflexive rather than effortful.

**3. Punishing premature pawn advances.**
Your opponent's 12.b4 is a classic case of a flank pawn lunge that weakens the center and leaves pieces uncoordinated. You punished it perfectly here. Drill tactical patterns where the opponent advances a flank pawn prematurely (b4/g4/h4 type blunders) — your 0.8-second reaction on 12...Bxb4 suggests you already have good instincts here; sharpening this further will make you consistently dangerous against higher-rated opponents who overextend.

The problem is sequencing: opening the position with b4 hands Black a free tempo to take on b4 with the bishop, exploiting the exposed king immediately. The best move O-O gets the king to safety first, and only then does the line show White calmly playing a3 to push the bishop back to b6, followed by Qe2 and Be3 to complete development with a stable position. The takeaway: never initiate pawn breaks or open the game while your king is uncastled — king safety is a prerequisite, not an afterthought.

summary:
## How the game went

You opened with the Queen's Gambit and handled the early moves well — 7.e4 was a confident central thrust (Best, +48.3% win rate), and lubo71 immediately stumbled with 7...Nge7 instead of the better b5, handing you roughly a 61% winning chance. You kept the advantage alive through moves 8 (Bxc4), 14 (Be3), and 15 (Qxb3), all rated Best. The game turned sharply on move 18: you played d5, a Blunder that shed 17 percentage points of win rate — Nd2 was correct, keeping queenside pressure with a line like Nd2 Qe7 f3 Qf7 Rfe1 Rac8. From there the position was already uneasy, but lubo71 gave it back with 20...Rc4 (Blunder, game back to 50/50); you missed the moment with 22.Nh4 (Rad1 was better), then 24.Re4 collapsed your position from 38% to 14%. You resigned on move 32 with only 4.2% winning chances.

---

## Strengths

- **Opening play was genuinely strong.** Six book moves followed by 7.e4 (played in under 2 seconds — instinct, not calculation) gave you a real advantage that most players at your level wouldn't find or wouldn't dare to take.
- **Calculation accuracy in the early middlegame.** Be3 and Qxb3 on moves 14–15 required concrete judgment, and you nailed both. Nine Best moves total across 32 moves is a solid ratio.
- **Coolheadedness under pressure.** Three moves were tagged coolheaded, showing you don't panic at the board.

---

## Weaknesses

**The d5 blunder (move 18) is the game's hinge.** You spent 98 seconds on it, which suggests you saw something but chose wrong. Nd2 was the correct plan — Nd2 Qe7 f3 Qf7 Rfe1 Rac8 h4 Bd7 keeps the position complex and in your favour. Instead, d5 threw away a 17-point win-rate lead and put you on the defensive immediately.

**You missed a gift on move 20.** After lubo71 played 20...Rc4 (a Blunder that swung the game back to even), you needed a precise response. You played Rfe1 (Inaccuracy) — h3 was already more accurate earlier that turn — and then compounded it two moves later with 22.Nh4 instead of Rad1 (c5 dxc6 Rxc6 h4 Qc7 Qg4 Qc8). Both Rad1 and h3 are moves that only 3–10% of players at your level find, so missing them is understandable — but it means you squandered the second life the opponent gave you.

**24.Re4 was the final nail.** Down to 38%, you played Re4 and fell to 14%. Re7 was the only move with any fighting chances (Re7 Qf5 Qg3 Qg6 Qe3 Rc8 Qe6 Rg8), but only 10% of your peers find it. By that point the position was already very difficult to save.

---

## What to train

1. **Pawn break discipline (specifically d5-type advances).** You clearly felt the urge to push d5 but misjudged the timing. Practice positions where a central pawn advance is tempting but premature — the key habit is asking "what does my opponent get for free after this?" before releasing the pawn. In this game, Nd2 first was the flexible move that kept options open.

2. **Rook activation in the middlegame.** Two of your three worst moves (Rfe1, Re4) involved misplacing or mistiming rooks. Rad1 on move 22 and Re7 on move 24 were both rook moves that required seeing a long-horizon plan. Drill rook-to-open-file and rook-lift exercises — your rook coordination let you down at the exact moments you needed it most.

3. **Converting opponent blunders.** lubo71 handed you the game back on move 20 with Rc4, but you couldn't capitalize. Practice "why did my opponent just do that?" as a reflex after every opponent move — especially in rapid games where the time exists to ask it. You spent 28 seconds on Rfe1 (the inaccuracy) but 99 seconds on Re4 later, suggesting your clock usage is reactive rather than proactive at critical junctures.

## How the Game Went

You opened with the Caro-Kann Advance and showed genuine understanding early on — 9...f6, 17...Qg5, and 18...Qxf4 were all best moves, keeping you competitive against a lower-rated opponent. At move 15, though, you chose 15...gxf5 over the superior exf5 — a recapture direction that 81% of players at your level get right, and that cost you nearly 10 win-percentage points. The game ground into a complex endgame where the position was roughly balanced, but move 38 proved fatal: 38...Bc4 (a blunder dropping win% to 11%) handed SergiuChiriac a decisive advantage. Ironically, the opponent returned the favor immediately by missing 39.bxc4+, a move 63% at this level would find, letting your win% briefly rebound to ~50%. But 39...b5 (another blunder, when 45% find 39...h6) threw that lifeline straight back, and from that point the game never recovered — you resigned on move 64.

---

## Strengths

**Opening solidity and cool head.** Four "coolheaded" tags and a string of best moves in the opening speak for themselves. Playing 9...f6 (36.7s of thought — you took your time), then 13...Kxg7 and 17...Qg5 without flinching shows you're not rattled by complications. 18...Qxf4 is flagged as "a natural move for your level," meaning your tactical instincts are sound when the position is sharp and readable.

**Accuracy that should win.** At 89.2% versus SergiuChiriac's 89.5%, this game was essentially even in quality. Against a 1310-rated player, you *should* be converting positions like this. The loss wasn't about being outplayed systematically — it was about two clustered blunders at a single critical moment.

---

## Weaknesses

**15...gxf5 — a pawn structure decision you should own.** Stockfish prefers exf5, keeping a more compact structure (the line exf5 Qc1 Ne4 shows the e4-knight becoming powerful). This is flagged as something 81% of players at your level get right. At 50 seconds on the clock — your longest think in the game — you clearly sensed it mattered. That makes it more important to study, not less.

**20...Kf6+ — a split-second slip.** You spent only 2.2 seconds here. Kf8+ was the correct square, and it's genuinely hard (only 6% find it), but the fact that you barely thought on a king move in a sharp position is worth noting.

**The endgame collapse at moves 38–39.** This is the heart of the loss. After 38...Bc4, the engine-preferred plan was the pawn advance 38...h5 — getting your kingside pawns moving (Nh3 Bd7 Nf2 Kc6 h4 Kb5 Nh1 follows). Instead, the bishop move miscoordinated your pieces. Then on move 39, after the opponent's error let you back to 50/50, you needed 39...h6 — again, a quiet pawn move preparing the h-pawn advance — but played 39...b5 instead. Both blunders point to the same underlying problem: in the endgame you gravitated toward pawn pushes on the wrong wing when the kingside breakthrough was the winning plan.

---

## What to Train

**1. Pawn recapture direction in the Caro-Kann.** The choice between gxf5 and exf5 is a recurring structural decision in the Advance Variation and its siblings. Study a handful of representative positions and ask yourself what each recapture achieves (open g-file vs. strong central pawn). Since 81% of your peers get this right, it's within your reach — you just need to make the logic explicit rather than intuitive.

**2. Endgame pawn-break identification.** Moves 38 and 39 both had the same correct answer: advance the h-pawn. Practice recognizing positions where a flank pawn majority becomes the primary winning plan, especially when your pieces feel tangled. The pattern here — bishop temporarily sidelined, knight awkwardly placed — is common. Train yourself to ask "which pawns can move?" before deciding on piece maneuvers.

**3. Slow down on king moves.** 20...Kf6+ at 2.2 seconds is the outlier in an otherwise thoughtful game (your average was 13.4s, and your best moves came after longer thinks). King moves in the middlegame and early endgame are high-stakes and often have only one right square. Make it a habit to spend at least your average time on any king move — Kf8+ was accessible if you'd paused to check both squares.

summary:
## How the game went

You entered the Caro-Kann Accelerated Panov and navigated the opening solidly — eight book moves, then three consecutive best/great decisions in the middlegame: 18...Nxc7 and 19...Qxd4 kept the position near equality. The first slip came at move 22 with 22...Bd8 (better was 22...Bf6, heading toward Bf6 Qf3 Rad8 and active counterplay) and the game slowly tilted toward PhilTour37. At move 25 you played 25...f6, a mistake that dropped your winning chances to 20% — Qf5, leading to Qf5 Qa4 b5 Qb4 Qd5 a4 bxa4 Qxa4, was the fighting defence. Remarkably, PhilTour37 handed the initiative back with 26.Bc7 (a mistake; the engine prefers Qb5), but you didn't capitalise: 27...Bxc7+ (better was the centralising Qe5+, which after g3 Bxc7 Qxc7 Qxc7 Rxc7 Rb8 b3 still gave real chances) left you at just 18.8% winning chances, and the hasty 28...Qxf2 — played in only 7 seconds — was the game-ending blunder, collapsing your chances to 0.1% instantly. Qe5+, forcing Qxe5 fxe5 Ra5 Rf8 Kg3 h5 f3, still offered a difficult but playable ending.

---

## Strengths

Your opening preparation is clearly solid — the Caro-Kann book phase was clean and you knew your lines. The middlegame sequence of 18...Nxc7 (Great) and 19...Qxd4 (Best) showed real tactical awareness: you spotted and executed the piece consolidation accurately. 12...O-O and 14...Qxd7 were also best-in-class choices, confirming that when the position is straightforward and you have time, your move quality is well above your rating. You also grabbed a real opportunity when PhilTour37 erred on move 26 — unfortunately the follow-up didn't match.

---

## Weaknesses

The critical flaw is time management under pressure. Your two worst moves — 27...Bxc7+ and 28...Qxf2 — were both played in 7 seconds, at exactly the moment the game required the most care. Qe5+ was the right idea on *both* of those moves, but you either didn't look for it or dismissed it too quickly. The pattern is telling: earlier at move 22 you spent 38.8 seconds on 22...Bd8, a less critical moment, while burning through the decisive sequence in 14 seconds combined. Beyond the time issue, 25...f6 shows a tendency to play reactive, pawn-push "consolidating" moves in bad positions instead of seeking active queen counterplay with Qf5 — a harder but far more resilient approach.

---

## What to train

**1. Queen centralisation under pressure.** Qe5+ was the computer's top choice on three consecutive moves (25, 27, 28) and you never found it. Practice tactical puzzles specifically involving centralised queen checks that create perpetual or trade-down opportunities from a slightly worse position — this pattern (active queen vs. retreating pawns) is clearly your blind spot right now.

**2. Speed calibration in the endgame.** You averaged 15.5 seconds per move overall but compressed your two decisive choices into 7 seconds each. In your next rapid games, consciously set a floor: if you're down material or your position is under threat, spend *more* time, not less. Blitz habits creeping into rapid are what turned a 90% accuracy game into a loss by checkmate.

**3. Bishop vs. queen activity trade-offs.** Both 22...Bd8 and 27...Bxc7+ chose bishop activity over centralising the queen. The engine's preferred lines in both cases began with the queen move. This suggests a positional habit of overvaluing bishops — worth reviewing a few Caro-Kann games specifically focused on when to prioritise queen activity over bishop development.

summary:
## How the game went

The Slav Defense: Three Knights Variation started reasonably for you through seven book moves, but the middlegame quickly became turbulent. An aggressive 8.e5 — played after a full two minutes of thought — was already a mistake, and 11.Nxd5 (another long think, nearly 90 seconds) was a blunder that collapsed your winning chances from around 55% to 27%. The position continued to deteriorate: 15.f4 and 19.fxe5 pushed your win probability as low as 11–14%, yet you stayed in the fight by finding four consecutive best moves — 16.Qxd5, 18.Kh1, 23.Bxe5, and 25.h3 — that kept gigimark76 under just enough pressure. The decisive moment came deep in the endgame at move 51: your opponent, with a near-winning resource in 51...Nc5+, instead played the weaker 51...Rh3+, missing a ~48% swing in evaluation. Then 57...Kd2 was a clean blunder that ended the game, and gigimark76 resigned.

---

## Strengths

- **Resilience and cool head.** You took three blunders and mistakes early, watched your eval crater to ~11%, and never panicked — Stockfish tagged you "coolheaded" three times. That matters more than accuracy in a rough middlegame.
- **Consecutive best moves under pressure.** Moves 16–25 form the backbone of the win. Finding 16.Qxd5 (win% 18.5), 18.Kh1 (natural but correctly avoiding back-rank danger), 23.Bxe5, and 25.h3 in sequence while your position was objectively losing is exactly the kind of "don't make it worse" precision that converts games against higher-rated opponents.
- **Overall accuracy above your rating.** 90.0% against an 1440 — a solid 73-point upset — and the accuracy gap (90.0 vs 87.2) ultimately favored you.

---

## Weaknesses

- **Long thinks that still end in blunders.** 11.Nxd5 cost nearly 90 seconds and 27 win-percentage points; 8.e5 cost 129 seconds and was still a mistake. Spending that much time and arriving at the wrong move suggests the evaluation framework, not the calculation speed, is the problem. The engine's recommendation, 11.Qxd4 (line: Qxd4 dxc4 Qxc4 Ngxe5 Qa4 Nc6 Rd1 Be7), would have kept the game comfortably balanced — you were looking for a tactical shot where a quiet recentralizing queen move was correct.
- **Misjudging pawn breaks.** Three of your five worst moves involve pushing pawns aggressively (e5, f4, fxe5). The engine's preferred lines for 15.f4 and 19.fxe5 both start with more conservative rook and piece moves (Re1 and fxg5 respectively). There's a pattern of choosing pawn activity when piece coordination is the better plan.
- **Quick decisions on captures.** 21.Bxg5 took only 10.6 seconds and was a mistake; the engine preferred 21.Qxg5 (line: Qxg5 Rg8 Qxe5 Nxe5 g3 Nd3 Rf5 Rde8). Blunder-checking capture-recapture sequences deserves more clock time than piece moves.

---

## What to train

1. **Queen centralization vs. tactical jumping.** The Nxd5 blunder and the Bxg5/Qxg5 mix-up both reflect the same bias: preferring an active piece leap over a quiet, centralizing move. Drill positions where the engine's top choice is a queen or rook redeployment rather than a capture — this is specifically where your pattern library needs filling at this level.

2. **Pawn-break evaluation before committing.** e5, f4, fxe5 all misfired in this game. Before pushing a pawn, run a quick mental checklist: does it open lines for your pieces or the opponent's? In each of those three cases the pawn push opened lines that helped gigimark76. Practice middlegame puzzles tagged "pawn structure" where the correct answer is *not* advancing — recognizing when to hold the tension is a level-up skill.

3. **Endgame conversion with a material edge.** You won here because your opponent blundered, not because the endgame was clean — the position around moves 51–57 was close to losing for you before 51...Rh3+ let you escape. Spend time on basic rook-and-pawn and minor-piece endgames: knowing the theoretical winning and drawing zones would let you build toward those positions deliberately rather than relying on the opponent to err first.

summary:
## How the game went

This was a crisp 15-move miniature in the Caro-Kann Classical Variation. flyingcocona began going astray early: on move 6 the knight leap **Ng5** was a mistake — the engine preferred **Bf4**, after which play would continue **Nf6 Nxf6+ gxf6 Qd2 Qb6 O-O-O Na6** with a more balanced fight; Ng5 immediately handed you a meaningful edge (White's win% fell to 37.6%). Then on move 8, **d5** compounded the problem — the steadier **Nf2**, with the idea of **Nbd7 Bd3 O-O O-O c5 dxc5 Bxc5**, could still hold, but d5 dropped White's winning chances to 25.3%. You converted with clinical precision: **10...Ne3 — 11...Nc2+ — 13...Bxe3+ — 14...h5**, four consecutive engine-best moves that steered the position to a forced checkmate on move 15.

---

## Strengths

**Tactical alertness.** You spotted the knight maneuver Ne3 → Nc2+ (a check) and played it almost instantly — 6.3s and 9.3s respectively. From that moment you never let go, pushing the win probability from 82.8% to 89.5% to 95.3% to 96.7% across your final four moves. Zero evaluation drops on your side is the headline: you made no errors at all in this game.

**Calculation speed under fire.** The most dangerous moments in tactical games are the transitions — you handled all of them quickly and correctly. Bxe3+ took just 1.0s. You were clearly calculating several moves ahead.

**Opening preparation.** Five book moves meant you arrived at the first independent position on familiar ground, which almost certainly gave you the confidence to see the coming tactical motif before White even set it up.

---

## Weaknesses

There is genuinely nothing to criticize in this game. You played at 94.7% accuracy with five best moves and zero blunders, mistakes, or inaccuracies. The honest caveat is scope: at 15 moves, the game ended before any endgame, deep calculation under time trouble, or long conversion was required. This result proves your tactical eye is sharp — it doesn't yet tell us much about your technique across 50 moves. That's not a flaw in this game; it's just what a miniature can and can't reveal.

---

## What to train

**1. Study what White should do in the Caro-Kann Classical.** flyingcocona's Ng5 and d5 were errors precisely because they mishandled this structure. The engine's answer was Bf4 → Nf6 Nxf6+ gxf6 Qd2 Qb6 O-O-O Na6 — that's the real battleground you'll face against stronger opponents. Knowing those plans from Black's side will let you press for active counterplay rather than simply waiting for a gift.

**2. Build your knight-maneuver pattern library.** The Ne3 → Nc2+ motif that won this game belongs to a family of deep knight-hop patterns — pieces that travel through multiple squares to land on a fork or a harassing check square. You already have the instinct for these; now drill puzzles specifically focused on long-range knight tours and knight forks with check. The goal is to recognize the destination square two or three moves earlier.

**3. Practice converting slow advantages.** This game ended in a miniature — brilliant, but flyingcocona essentially handed it to you. Your real growth edge is winning positions where the opponent defends for 35–45 moves. Work on technical conversion: rook endgames, bishop-vs.-knight imbalances, passed pawn technique. The tactical engine is clearly running; the endgame engine needs the road time.

summary:
## How the Game Went

This was an exceptionally brief encounter — all four moves were played within the Queen's Gambit Accepted: Central Variation, Alekhine System, and Djamal2019 resigned immediately after your 4.Nc3. Both sides played entirely within book, so the engine saw no errors from either player. The position after 4.Nc3 was essentially balanced (win% 47.2%), meaning the resignation had nothing to do with the position itself — it was almost certainly a premature concession, possibly an accidental click or a connection dropout on Black's side.

## Strengths

You came prepared: every single move (all four) was a book move, which is exactly right for a rated classical game. Staying in theory confidently, even on a 3.3s average per move, signals familiarity with this line.

## Weaknesses

Four moves is not enough to identify any real weakness. The position was objectively equal when the opponent resigned. There were no errors, no missed tactics, and no strategic decisions to evaluate. This game tells you nothing about your actual play.

## What to Train

1. **Know where your opening leads.** The QGA Alekhine System (4.Nc3) typically produces rich middlegames with an IQP or hanging pawn structures for Black. Since you play this line, study the typical plans and pawn structures that arise a dozen moves in — your book preparation ends right where the real decisions begin.

2. **Don't let short wins feel like evidence of strength.** A 4-move resignation gives you a point but zero feedback. Make a habit of reviewing only games that lasted long enough for both sides to make genuine decisions (roughly 20+ moves), so your training is grounded in real errors.

3. **Use classical time more actively.** With 7.4 seconds per move and only 4 moves played, there was no time pressure — but classical games are where deep calculation should happen. In your next proper classical game, try to spend significantly more time on critical decisions; that investment is what separates classical from blitz play.

moves.comment: Nc6 develops but cedes the center; e5 was stronger — after Nf3 exd4 Nxd4 Bc5, Black develops with tempo against the knight while keeping a grip on the center, worth nearly 10% win-rate.
moves.deep: By playing Nc6, Black develops a piece but does nothing to contest White's pawns on d4 and e4, which together form a dominant center. White is free to consolidate and expand while Black's only material compensation — the pawn on c4 — is structural rather than dynamic. The idea behind 4...e5 is immediate central confrontation: after Nf3 exd4 Nxd4, Black has eliminated the d4 pawn entirely, breaking up the two-pawn center rather than coexisting with it. Then Bc5 attacks the knight that recaptured on d4, forcing White to spend a tempo on Be3 to defend it — as the line shows — while Black completes development with O-O on a fully open e-file. The takeaway is that when you have already captured a wing pawn (c4) and your opponent holds a full center with d4+e4, routine development does not equalize — you need active central counterplay right away. The 9.5 win% gap between Nc6 and e5 reflects exactly this: Nc6 concedes the center, e5 tears it apart.

summary:
## How the Game Went

The English Defense opened quietly, with eight moves of book theory from both sides. You then seized the initiative with a composed sequence — **12.dxc5** and **13.Bxa6**, both engine-best and marked coolheaded — which pressured dodeja27's queenside just as he blundered with **13...Nxa6** (Rxa6 was correct and kept the loss margin around 10%; Nxa6 handed you a genuine edge). You converted immediately with the lightning-fast **14.Bxa5** (Great, just 1.1 seconds) and consolidated with **15.b4**, pushing your win probability to a peak of **65.6%**. From that high point everything unraveled: **16.Rfd1** bled nearly 12% of your advantage despite a four-minute think, and the fatal **23.Ne7+** collapsed the position entirely — from uncertain to resignable in a single move, finishing with **25.Qb7** and the flag down two moves later.

---

## Strengths

The moves-12-through-15 sequence was your best chess of the game. Capturing on c5, snatching the a6 bishop, following up on a5, and locking in with b4 — that's a four-move chain of connected decisions that correctly converted dodeja27's queenside mistake into a concrete material and pawn-structure edge. The **coolheaded** tag appearing five times across the game reflects a genuine ability to keep calm and find precise trades under pressure. Your overall 90.6% accuracy also shows the game was not plagued by constant drift — the damage was concentrated in two specific moments.

---

## Weaknesses

**Move 16 — the long think that chose wrong.** You spent nearly **224 seconds** on Rfd1 — by far your longest think of the game — and still landed on an inaccuracy. The engine preferred **Nd4**, a centralising leap that attacks the knight on b7 and keeps the queens active (Nd4 Nb7 Bxc7 Qd7 b5 e5 Qc6 Qxc6 kept your edge at -9.4%). Rfd1 is a reasonable-looking developing move, but it lets dodeja27 reorganise and trims your advantage from 65% down to 54%. Investing that much clock without seeing the better option is a pattern worth examining.

**Move 23 — the decisive mistake.** With your win probability already fragile, **Ne7+** dropped it to just **11%**. The correct path was **Bxc3**, followed by Qxc3 Qxc3 Nxc3 Re1 Kg7 — an exchange sequence that, while still a loss at -38.5%, keeps the game competitive rather than over. Ne7+ looks active (a check, a fork threat), but it throws away the piece coordination needed to stay in the fight. You spent 82 seconds here and chose the visually aggressive move over the objectively solid one.

---

## What to Train

**1. Converting a structural advantage without concrete tactics.** Moves 15-16 show a gap between building an edge and knowing *what to do next* when there's no forcing sequence. The Nd4 idea at move 16 is "quiet" — no checks, no captures — but it dominates space and ties down the opponent. Practice positions where you have a pawn or structure edge and must find the best non-tactical follow-up.

**2. Recognising when a check is a distraction.** Ne7+ on move 23 is the classic trap: your hand reaches for a check because it *looks* active. But Bxc3 — a quiet capture that trades a piece and removes a strong pawn — was the way to fight on. Drill tactical puzzles where the best move is not a check or immediate capture, to train your eye past the most visually striking option.

**3. Clock management on critical decisions.** You burned 224 seconds on move 16 and still didn't find the best move. Then on move 23 you spent 82 seconds on the decisive error. Long thinks are fine, but they should be spent calculating concrete variations — the Nd4 line is only four moves deep and fully verifiable in that time. Practice calculating one or two candidate moves exhaustively rather than deliberating over the position's general character.

summary:
## How the Game Went

You played Black in the Caro-Kann Advance, Botvinnik-Carls Defense against crocodile2u (1376) and lost by resignation on move 52. The opening was your strongest phase — after seven book moves you found 11...b5 (Best), 13...Bxb5 (Great), and the rare 14...Qa5 (Best, played by only 6% at your rating), keeping the game sharp and competitive. The real pivot came at moves 23-24: crocodile2u blundered consecutively with 23.Re6 and 24.a5, but the critical reply Nfxd4 — a central pawn capture activating your knights — slipped by twice, and you played the calmer Bb7 then Nd8 instead. Those missed chances allowed White to stabilize and press; by move 32 you played Nc4 in under 4 seconds, missing the better Nxe8, and the game wound down to your resignation twenty moves later.

---

## Strengths

- **Precise opening play.** Seven book moves followed by the b5–Bxb5–Qa5 sequence was genuinely impressive. Qa5 is a rare, above-your-level choice and you spent 147 seconds finding it — that's calculation, not luck.
- **Resilience after your first mistake.** After 18...f6 (a mistake), you responded with 19...Qc8, which was still the engine's best option. Keeping your composure after a slip and finding the right defensive resource is a real skill.
- **Consistency throughout.** Sixteen Excellent moves, eleven Good, seven Best — your 89.3% accuracy reflects a game that was mostly well-handled. The loss came from a few concentrated moments, not general sloppiness.

---

## Weaknesses

- **Missed Nfxd4 on moves 23 and 24.** This is the clearest missed opportunity in the game. Your opponent handed you consecutive blunders (Re6, then a5), and both times the engine's answer was Nfxd4 — a sequence beginning Nfxd4 cxd4 Nxd4, activating your knights in the center. You played Bb7 (inaccuracy) then Nd8 (inaccuracy) instead. Stockfish reports only 1-2% of players your rating find this move — so it's not a simple miss — but two opportunities in a row to seize the initiative both slipped by.
- **18...f6: wrong answer after a long think.** You spent 70 seconds here and still chose a mistake. The engine prefers the knight maneuver Na5 (→ Na5 Na3 Bc4 Bf4 h6 Nf3 Bb3 Nb5), which only 12% of players at your level find. Spending over a minute and choosing incorrectly suggests you were calculating the right number of moves but starting from the wrong candidate — f6 was likely your first instinct, and the clock ran on it rather than on genuinely different plans.
- **32...Nc4: blunder in 3.7 seconds.** The starkest moment in the game. After 32.Bf4 (your opponent's mistake, which gave you a brief window), you responded instantly with Nc4 — a blunder. The correct reply was Nxe8, beginning Nxe8 a6 Bc8 Qa4 Qf7 Nd2 Bd8 Nb3. Under 4 seconds in a position that required a concrete decision is the classic "I'm losing, let me just do something" reflex, and it accelerated the defeat.

---

## What to Train

1. **Tactical pattern: central pawn captures to activate pieces.** The move Nfxd4 was the answer on two consecutive turns and you missed it both times. This specific motif — capturing a central pawn with a knight to open lines and gain tempo — is underrepresented in your pattern library. Drill positions where accepting a pawn with a knight triggers a piece activity cascade. It came up twice in one game; it will come up again.

2. **Candidate move discipline on long thinks.** The gap between move 18 (70 seconds, wrong) and move 32 (3.7 seconds, blunder) tells the same story from opposite ends: your calculation process isn't reliably surfacing the right moves before the clock runs. Practice explicitly listing 2-3 candidate moves before calculating any of them. On move 18, Na5 needed to be on the list; it almost certainly wasn't. A bad candidate never gets calculated, no matter how long you think.

3. **Converting opponent errors under pressure.** Crocodile2u gave you real winning chances at moves 23 and 24 — and you were already in a disadvantaged position emotionally and on the eval bar. When you sense your opponent has erred, train yourself to pause and look for the most forcing, active continuation (captures, central breaks) before defaulting to a safe-looking but passive move. The pattern of Bb7 and Nd8 both being plausible-looking retreats that missed the point is worth replaying in your own analysis.

moves.comment: Re8+ wastes momentum; a6 was far stronger, pushing the passer immediately with Ba8 Bd7 to follow.
moves.deep: Re8+ forces an exchange but simplifying here dissipates white's most powerful asset. a6 advances the passed pawn immediately, and after Ba8, the bishop is banished to a completely passive corner. Bd7 then attacks the rook, Rd8 Bxf5 wins the f5 knight, and Bh4 g3 shows white keeping full control. The lesson is that a far-advanced passed pawn must be pushed before the opponent's pieces can blockade it — every tempo spent on side-activity instead is a missed winning chance.

moves.comment: Natural and nearly optimal recapture.
Best recapture, maintaining a large advantage.
Best defensive centralization, though rare at this level.
Bf4 is a serious mistake; a6 was winning with Bh5 Ne4 Qa4 to follow, but Bf4 lets black back in.
moves.deep: Bf4 activates the bishop but ignores the real winning plan. a6 advances the pawn to the seventh rank again; after Ba8, the bishop is completely boxed in, and then Bh5 Ne4 Qa4 Qf8 Be1 Ng7 shows white's pieces coordinating to restrain black's counterplay while the pawn marches. Bf4 gives black extra moves to organize around the knights and delays the inevitable queening. The passed pawn is white's trump card — every move that does not advance or support it weakens the advantage.

moves.comment: Nc4 is a blunder; Nxe8 was necessary to recapture the bishop and keep fighting via a6 Bc8 Qa4 Qf7.
moves.deep: Nc4 attacks the queen but wins no material and misses the immediately winning Nxe8, which captures a piece outright. After Nxe8 a6 Bc8, black has won the bishop and can continue fighting: Qa4 Qf7 Nd2 Bd8 Nb3 shows black maintaining piece coordination and real defensive resources. Nc4 simply chases the queen with no concrete gain, allowing white to keep full control with the dangerous passed pawn. When a piece capture is available, always calculate it first before choosing a less forcing alternative.
